parentQuestion: A patient reports being able to 'feel' someone touching their left hand, but cannot localize WHERE on the hand they are being touched or identify the shape of objects placed in that hand. The lesion is in the ___.

options: The cortical area that detects basic touch sensations
scaffolds.explain: PRIMARY SOMATOSENSORY CORTEX (S1) is in the postcentral gyrus and DETECTS basic touch sensations — pressure, texture, temperature, vibration. Raw sensory data.

wrongExplains.0: Movement planning is prefrontal/premotor, not S1.
wrongExplains.2: Memory storage involves hippocampus and cortex, not S1.
wrongExplains.3: Vision is processed in occipital lobe, not S1.

scaffolds.q: What is the somatosensory association cortex?
options: The area that interprets touch — localizing and identifying objects by feel
scaffolds.explain: SOMATOSENSORY ASSOCIATION CORTEX interprets what S1 detects — WHERE you're being touched and WHAT you're touching. It builds meaning from raw sensation.

wrongExplains.0: Association cortex processes differently than primary cortex.
wrongExplains.2: Motor cortex controls movement, not sensation.
wrongExplains.3: Visual cortex processes vision, not touch.

options: Inability to identify objects by touch despite intact sensation
scaffolds.explain: ASTEREOGNOSIS is inability to IDENTIFY OBJECTS BY TOUCH despite INTACT SENSATION. 'A-' = without, 'stereo' = solid, 'gnosis' = knowledge. Can feel but can't recognize shapes.

wrongExplains.0: Astereognosis affects touch recognition, not vision.

scaffolds.q: If S1 is damaged, what happens?
options: Loss of basic touch sensation — can't feel touch at all
scaffolds.explain: S1 DAMAGE causes LOSS of BASIC TOUCH SENSATION — the patient can't FEEL touch at all on the affected area. Primary detection fails completely.

wrongExplains.0: Astereognosis is from association damage, not S1.
wrongExplains.2: S1 damage causes total sensation loss, not just localization.
wrongExplains.3: S1 damage causes total loss, not selective recognition problems.

scaffolds.q: What does it mean to 'localize' touch?
options: Knowing WHERE on your body you are being touched
scaffolds.explain: LOCALIZING touch means knowing WHERE on your body you're being touched — which finger, which part of the hand. This requires association cortex processing.

wrongExplains.0: Pain detection is different from localization.
wrongExplains.2: Movement is motor function, not sensory localization.

scaffolds.q: What pattern indicates association cortex damage?
options: Can feel touch but can't localize it or identify objects by feel
scaffolds.explain: ASSOCIATION CORTEX DAMAGE pattern: CAN FEEL touch (S1 intact) but CAN'T LOCALIZE it or IDENTIFY objects by feel. Raw sensation works; interpretation fails.

wrongExplains.0: Total sensation loss indicates S1 damage, not association.

scaffolds.q: Where is the somatosensory association cortex located?
options: Posterior parietal lobe, just behind S1
scaffolds.explain: SOMATOSENSORY ASSOCIATION CORTEX is in the POSTERIOR PARIETAL LOBE, just behind S1. It integrates and interprets the raw touch data from S1.

wrongExplains.0: The frontal lobe handles motor and executive functions.
wrongExplains.3: The temporal lobe processes hearing and memory.

scaffolds.q: Can feel touch + can't localize or identify = what damage?
scaffolds.explain: SOMATOSENSORY ASSOCIATION CORTEX damage. S1 is intact (can feel), but interpretation fails (can't localize or identify). Classic association area deficit.

wrongExplains.0: S1 damage would cause loss of all touch sensation.
wrongExplains.2: Motor cortex damage affects movement, not sensation.
wrongExplains.3: Visual cortex damage affects vision, not touch.

scaffolds.q: Which area is damaged if touch is felt but can't be localized or used to identify objects?
scaffolds.explain: SOMATOSENSORY ASSOCIATION CORTEX. The pattern — intact sensation but impaired localization and object identification — indicates association cortex damage causing astereognosis.

wrongExplains.0: S1 damage causes loss of basic sensation, not interpretation.
wrongExplains.2: Motor cortex damage affects movement, not sensory interpretation.
wrongExplains.3: Auditory cortex processes hearing, not touch.
